tasks.title: Add request profiling middleware and capture Phase 2 baseline
tasks.description: Instrument the FastAPI app so every request logs SQL query count, total query time, total handler time, and response size. This is the measurement baseline for every other task — without it you cannot tell which subsequent change moved the needle.
tasks.details:
CONTEXT: Phase 1 (already shipped) eliminated CTE-over-route_aircraft scans across 10 endpoints in 7 files. Added indexes idx_ra_route_profit, idx_ra_od_valid_profit, idx_ra_origin_valid_profit. Wired ANALYZE into migration. Replaced UPPER(TRIM(iata)) antipattern with upfront IATA→ID resolution. Headline win: /api/fleet/summary query dropped from 88s to 0.05s (~1700x). All Phase 1 fixed queries now show SEARCH USING COVERING INDEX with sub-millisecond plans. Phase 2 targets the remaining bottlenecks: cold-cache I/O, per-request connection overhead, frontend payload weight.

SUCCESS CRITERIA for all of Phase 2 (verified in task 7):
- Cold dashboard page load < 1s for any single-hub view
- Warm dashboard page load < 200ms for any view
- /api/fleet/summary cold hit drops from 11.5s baseline to < 1s
- Total page weight < 100KB on initial load (excluding HTMX)
- No 'database is locked' errors under 20 concurrent requests
- WAL mode confirmed active on the production DB

IMPLEMENTATION:

Add a FastAPI middleware in dashboard/middleware/profiling.py that wraps each request and logs: route path, method, status, total_ms, db_ms, query_count, response_bytes. Use sqlite3 connection's set_trace_callback or wrap the cursor to count queries and accumulate timings on a contextvar so async + threadpool handlers both work.

Also add an optional ?debug=1 query param that injects a JSON trailer in an HTML comment with the same metrics, visible in browser devtools.

Optionally drop in pyinstrument as a dev-only middleware behind env var PROFILE=1 for full call-stack flamegraphs on the slowest endpoints.

BEFORE making any other change in this PRD, capture baseline timings for these 6 pages and record them in PRD/perf-baseline-phase2.md:
  - / (index)
  - /buy-next?hub=LAX
  - /my-fleet (hits /api/fleet/summary cold and warm)
  - /my-routes
  - /hub-explorer (or whatever Hub Explorer's actual route is)
  - /contribution-optimizer

For each page, record: cold first hit (after fresh dashboard restart), three subsequent warm hits, total page weight from devtools Network tab, and time-to-first-paint. These six pages are the basis for verifying success criteria in task 7.
tasks.testStrategy: Hit each baseline page once with debug=1 and confirm metrics appear in logs. Save before/after table to PRD/perf-baseline-phase2.md. Confirm pyinstrument flamegraph generates correctly when PROFILE=1.

tasks.title: Apply SQLite performance pragmas at connection open
tasks.description: Configure SQLite for the dashboard's read-heavy single-user workload. Phase 1 confirmed an 11.5s cold-cache cliff on /api/fleet/summary even after the index fix — these pragmas are the direct mitigation. Expect cold loads to drop into the sub-second range.
tasks.details:
In whichever module opens dashboard connections (likely database/connection.py or similar), execute these pragmas immediately after every sqlite3.connect():

  PRAGMA journal_mode=WAL;
  PRAGMA synchronous=NORMAL;
  PRAGMA cache_size=-200000;     -- 200MB page cache (negative = KB)
  PRAGMA mmap_size=268435456;    -- 256MB memory-mapped I/O
  PRAGMA temp_store=MEMORY;
  PRAGMA busy_timeout=5000;      -- 5s lock wait instead of immediate fail

NOTES:
- WAL mode persists on the DB file, so it only needs to be set once, but setting on every open is idempotent and harmless.
- WAL creates .db-wal and .db-shm sidecar files next to the main DB. Add both to .gitignore.
- mmap_size=256MB lets SQLite map the hottest portion of the DB into the process's address space, eliminating the syscall overhead that's causing the cold cliff.
- cache_size=-200000 holds 200MB of pages in memory; combined with mmap, the index pages stay hot across requests.
- Verify journal_mode actually switched by reading it back: SELECT * FROM pragma_journal_mode; — it can fail silently if the DB is on a network share or held by another process.

Do NOT enable synchronous=NORMAL on the extraction CLI's writer connection without testing. Extraction is rerunnable so it's probably fine, but make a deliberate decision rather than blanket-applying. The dashboard reader is the only target for this task.
tasks.testStrategy: After applying, run SELECT * FROM pragma_journal_mode; and confirm it returns 'wal'. Re-run the 6 baseline pages from task 1 cold (after fresh dashboard restart) and warm. Expected: /api/fleet/summary cold drops from 11.5s to < 1s. Other endpoints should see 30-60% cold improvement. Confirm .db-wal and .db-shm appear next to the database file.

tasks.title: Use long-lived connections instead of per-request connect()
tasks.description: Eliminate per-request sqlite3.connect() overhead, which is non-trivial on Windows NTFS — typically 5-30ms per call. SQLite is fine with a single shared connection for read-mostly FastAPI workloads when WAL is enabled (task 2 prerequisite).
tasks.details:
Open one sqlite3.Connection at FastAPI startup, store it on app.state.db, and have all dashboard endpoints reach for app.state.db instead of opening fresh connections. Pass check_same_thread=False since FastAPI's run_in_threadpool dispatches handlers across threads — WAL + the GIL make this safe for read-mostly workloads.

For write paths (CSV imports, fleet add/edit, hub refresh), use a separate dedicated writer connection also stored on app.state, OR open a short-lived one per write. Writes are infrequent enough that the cost doesn't matter, and isolating them keeps the reader connection clean.

Close connections cleanly in the FastAPI shutdown event using @app.on_event('shutdown') or the lifespan context manager.

If you hit any 'database is locked' errors after this change, that's a signal you have a writer holding a transaction open longer than expected — fix the writer (commit promptly, use `with conn:` blocks), don't revert the pooling. The busy_timeout=5000 pragma from task 2 will absorb most transient contention.

Watch out: existing helpers like _my_fleet_rows, _airline_est_profit_from_my_routes, etc. probably take a conn parameter. Make sure they receive the shared connection rather than each opening their own. Grep for `sqlite3.connect(` in dashboard/ to find any stragglers.
tasks.testStrategy: Re-run baseline pages and confirm the per-request db_ms metric from task 1 drops by the connection-open overhead (~5-30ms per request on Windows). Stress-test with 20 parallel requests via `hey -n 200 -c 20 http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/fleet/summary` and confirm zero 'database is locked' errors. Confirm `pytest -x` still passes (the threading lock test that was already flaky may still flake — that's pre-existing).

tasks.title: Materialize YELLOW aggregate views into refresh-on-write cache tables (conditional)
tasks.description: Phase 1 identified several queries that are inherently full-aggregate and cannot be filtered: SELECT DISTINCT origin_id, COUNT(*) totals, top_hubs on the index page, and aggregates over v_best_routes. They're acceptable today but may become noticeable after WAL+pragmas drop everything else. Materialize them into small physical tables refreshed after extraction — but only if the profiler confirms they're still slow.
tasks.details:
CONDITIONAL TASK: Do NOT proceed until task 2 lands and task 1's profiler has measured these queries post-pragmas. If all YELLOW queries are < 50ms warm after task 2, mark this task as 'not needed' and skip.

Targets identified in Phase 1 as YELLOW:
  - pages.py: _origin_hub_iatas_for_fleet_plan, _hubs_with_names
  - pages.py: page_index stats (COUNT(*), COUNT(DISTINCT))
  - pages.py: page_index top_hubs (GROUP BY origin_id aggregate)
  - pages.py: page_contributions DISTINCT hub via v_best_routes
  - analytics.py: api_aircraft_cost_breakdown (full aggregate grouped by aircraft)

For each target still slow after task 2:

1. Create a parallel physical table:
     CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS dashboard_index_stats (
         key TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
         value_int INTEGER,
         value_text TEXT,
         updated_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
     );

   For tabular aggregates, mirror the source view's columns directly:
     CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS top_hubs_cache AS SELECT * FROM (top_hubs query) WHERE 1=0;

2. Add a refresh function in dashboard/services/cache.py that DELETEs and re-INSERTs each cache table, then ANALYZEs them and commits.

3. Wire refresh into:
   - End of `main.py extract` after extraction completes
   - End of any fleet import / fleet add / fleet edit endpoint
   - End of any my_routes import / edit endpoint
   - A new `main.py refresh-cache` subcommand for manual rebuild

4. Update the dashboard endpoints to read from the cache tables instead of the views. Keep the views around as the source-of-truth definition that the cache mirrors.
tasks.testStrategy: Profile each YELLOW query after task 2 lands. Only proceed with materialization for queries still > 50ms warm. For each materialized query, time before/after and confirm sub-10ms reads from cache. Run extraction end-to-end and confirm caches rebuild without errors and contain expected row counts. Manually edit a fleet row and confirm the relevant cache picks up the change.

tasks.title: Paginate and cap any remaining unbounded result sets (conditional)
tasks.description: Audit every endpoint that returns rows from route_aircraft or its derived caches. Anything returning more than ~200 rows wastes work in SQL serialization, JSON encoding, and HTMX rendering. Most of Phase 1's fixes already filter tightly, so this is a smaller pass than the original PRD anticipated.
tasks.details:
CONDITIONAL TASK: Spend at most 90 minutes on this. If no endpoint is returning > 500 rows after task 1's profiler runs, mark 'not needed' and skip.

Grep for endpoints that return list payloads:

  grep -rn 'return.*HTMLResponse\|return.*JSONResponse\|return templates' dashboard/routes/

For each one, look at the underlying query and ask: could this return > 200 rows? If yes:

- Add a hard LIMIT 200 (or smaller) to the SQL.
- Add ?page=N&page_size=M query params with sensible defaults (page_size=50, max=200).
- For Hub Explorer and Contribution Optimizer specifically, use HTMX's hx-trigger="revealed" pattern on a sentinel row at the bottom of the table to lazy-load the next page.
- For the Global Heatmap: do not return per-row data. Aggregate to grid cells server-side and return only the aggregated payload.
- For CSV export endpoints, keep them unpaginated but stream with FastAPI's StreamingResponse so memory doesn't balloon.

Do NOT paginate fleet, my_routes, or hubs endpoints — those datasets are bounded by Saqib's actual fleet (172 aircraft, 180 routes, 6 hubs) and small enough to return whole.

The Phase 1 IATA→ID resolution work means most endpoints already filter by a tight ID, so pagination is a safety net, not a primary optimization.
tasks.testStrategy: Capture response payload sizes (bytes) and row counts for each endpoint via task 1's profiler. Confirm no endpoint returns more than 200 rows in a single response. Test infinite scroll on Hub Explorer manually and confirm subsequent pages load. Confirm CSV exports stream correctly (memory should stay flat during a large export).

tasks.title: Replace Tailwind CDN with a compiled stylesheet
tasks.description: If the dashboard loads Tailwind via the CDN JIT script (cdn.tailwindcss.com), every page pays a 200-500ms penalty and ships ~400KB of JS to do work that should happen at build time. Compiled CSS is ~10KB and renders instantly. This task is independent of all the SQL work and can run in parallel.
tasks.details:
PRECHECK: Open the base template (likely templates/base.html or templates/_layout.html) and look for:

  <script src="https://cdn.tailwindcss.com"></script>

If that line exists, proceed. If it's already compiled, mark this task 'not needed' and skip.

Replace with a build step:

1. From the project root: npm init -y && npm install -D tailwindcss@latest
2. npx tailwindcss init — creates tailwind.config.js. Set the content array to scan all template and JS files:
     content: ['./templates/**/*.html', './dashboard/**/*.html', './static/js/**/*.js']
3. Create static/src/app.css with:
     @tailwind base;
     @tailwind components;
     @tailwind utilities;
4. Build: npx tailwindcss -i static/src/app.css -o static/dist/app.css --minify
5. In the base template, replace the CDN script tag with:
     <link rel="stylesheet" href="/static/dist/app.css">
6. Add npm scripts to package.json:
     "build:css": "tailwindcss -i static/src/app.css -o static/dist/app.css --minify"
     "watch:css": "tailwindcss -i static/src/app.css -o static/dist/app.css --watch"
7. Commit static/dist/app.css to git. It's a build artifact but small enough that committing it avoids needing Node on every machine that runs the dashboard. Document the rebuild step in README.

While touching the frontend layer, also:
- Add <link rel="preload"> for HTMX so it loads in parallel with the document.
- Confirm static files are served by FastAPI's StaticFiles mount at /static.
- Set Cache-Control: public, max-age=31536000, immutable on /static/dist/* responses.

Visually verify EVERY dashboard page after the swap. Tailwind's content scanner only includes classes it sees in the template files, so any class generated dynamically in JS or built via string concatenation will be missing from the output. If anything looks broken, add the affected classes to the safelist in tailwind.config.js.
tasks.testStrategy: Compare page weight and Time-to-First-Paint in browser devtools Network tab before/after. Expect total page weight to drop by ~400KB and FCP to drop by 200ms+. Visually inspect every dashboard page (Hub Explorer, Aircraft Comparison, Route Analyzer, Fleet Planner, Contribution Optimizer, Global Heatmap, My Fleet, My Routes, Buy Next) and confirm all Tailwind classes still resolve correctly. Confirm the build step is documented in README.

tasks.title: Verify gains against baseline and document final state
tasks.description: Close the loop: re-run the same 6 baseline pages from task 1, record new timings, and write up what actually worked.
tasks.details:
Re-hit each of the 6 baseline pages with ?debug=1 enabled, both cold (after fresh dashboard restart) and warm (third subsequent hit). Record total_ms, db_ms, query_count, and response_bytes for each. Compare against PRD/perf-baseline-phase2.md and write the diffs to PRD/perf-results-phase2.md as a single markdown table.

Verify each success criterion from task 1's context block explicitly:
- Cold < 1s for any single-hub view? Pass/Fail per page.
- Warm < 200ms for any view? Pass/Fail per page.
- /api/fleet/summary cold < 1s (down from 11.5s)? Pass/Fail.
- Total page weight < 100KB? Pass/Fail per page.
- 20 concurrent requests without 'database is locked'? Pass/Fail.
- WAL active? Pass/Fail.

If any endpoint is still slow (> 500ms warm or > 1.5s cold), run pyinstrument against it via PROFILE=1 and identify the remaining bottleneck. At this point it should be Python serialization or template rendering rather than SQL — which means a different class of fix:
  - Slow Jinja2 rendering → enable bytecode cache (jinja2.FileSystemBytecodeCache)
  - Slow JSON serialization → swap json for orjson in dashboard/utils/json.py
  - Slow attribute access on Row objects → switch to dict factories or namedtuples

These are out of scope for this PRD — log them as Phase 3 candidates.

Update README's troubleshooting / operations section with:
- The WAL pragmas and what they do
- The materialized cache refresh command (if task 4 was needed)
- The Tailwind build step (if task 6 was needed)
- The PROFILE=1 env var for pyinstrument flamegraphs
- The composite indexes added in Phase 1 + Phase 2 and their purposes
tasks.testStrategy: PRD/perf-results-phase2.md exists with before/after numbers for all 6 baseline pages. README updated with the new operational notes. Every success criterion is explicitly verified and marked pass/fail. Any remaining bottlenecks are documented as Phase 3 candidates rather than ignored.
